---
title: "Introducing openseespy-solvers"
date: 2026-06-06
categories: [opensees, python, hpc, gpu]
description: "A small Python package that connects SciPy-style sparse linear and eigen solvers to OpenSeesPy's PythonSparse system."
author: Gustavo A. Araújo R.
---

I released [`openseespy-solvers`](https://pypi.org/project/openseespy-solvers/), a Python package that provides SciPy-style sparse linear and eigen solvers for OpenSeesPy's [`PythonSparse`](https://opensees.github.io/OpenSeesDocumentation/user/manual/analysis/system/PythonSparse.html) system command.

The goal is simple: make it easier to experiment with solver backends while keeping the OpenSeesPy modeling workflow familiar.

## How it works

![Conceptual workflow for connecting OpenSeesPy's `PythonSparse` interface to external Python solver backends, including GPU-enabled sparse solves.](../../assets/images/posts/openseespy-solvers-python-solver.png){fig-alt="Schematic of OpenSees assembling residual and tangent matrices, passing them to a Python solver object, copying data to a GPU backend, solving for displacement increments, and returning the result to OpenSees." width="100%"}

OpenSeesPy still owns the finite-element model, the implicit integration loop, and the element state determination. During each analysis step, OpenSeesPy assembles the residual vector and tangent matrix, then passes those arrays through `PythonSparse` to a Python solver object.

That solver object can call a CPU backend such as SciPy, or copy the assembled arrays to a GPU backend such as CuPy or `nvmath.sparse`. Once the backend solves for the displacement increment, the solver writes the result back to OpenSeesPy so the analysis can update the trial state, check convergence, and continue.

## Install

The base package includes NumPy/SciPy CPU solvers and requires Python 3.12 or newer:

```bash
python -m pip install openseespy-solvers
```

If OpenSeesPy is not already installed in your environment, use the optional extra:

```bash
python -m pip install "openseespy-solvers[opensees]"
```

Optional extras are available for UMFPACK and NVIDIA GPU backends:

```bash
python -m pip install "openseespy-solvers[umfpack]"
python -m pip install "openseespy-solvers[cuda12]"
python -m pip install "openseespy-solvers[cuda13]"
```

See the [installation guide](https://openseespy-solvers.readthedocs.io/en/stable/installation/) for platform notes, especially for UMFPACK on Windows and CUDA environments.

## Quick Example

```python
import openseespy.opensees as ops
from openseespy_solvers.scipy import spsolve

solver = spsolve()

# after defining the OpenSeesPy model:
ops.system("PythonSparse", solver.to_openseespy())
ops.numberer("RCM")
ops.constraints("Plain")
ops.integrator("LoadControl", 1.0)
ops.algorithm("Linear")
ops.analysis("Static")
ops.analyze(1)
```

For eigen analysis:

```python
from openseespy_solvers.scipy import eigsh

eigensolver = eigsh(tol=1e-8)
eigenvalues = ops.eigen("PythonSparse", 5, eigensolver.to_openseespy())
```

## Solver Backends

The package currently wraps solver objects from:

- [`scipy.sparse.linalg`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/sparse.linalg.html) for CPU sparse linear and eigen solves
- [`cupyx.scipy.sparse.linalg`](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/stable/reference/scipy_sparse.html) for GPU sparse workflows through CuPy
- [`nvmath.sparse`](https://developer.nvidia.com/nvmath-python) for GPU direct sparse solves

For more info, refer to the documentation and other links below.

## Links

- [Documentation](https://openseespy-solvers.readthedocs.io/en/stable/)
- [PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/openseespy-solvers/)
- [Source code](https://github.com/gaaraujo/openseespy-solvers)
- [Issues](https://github.com/gaaraujo/openseespy-solvers/issues)

This is an early release, and I am especially interested in feedback from OpenSeesPy users working with larger sparse systems, eigen analyses, and GPU-enabled environments.
